---
title: "Using because-py via R (reticulate)"
description: |
  How to use the NumPyro engine from R through the because package and reticulate.
  All examples use the Rhinogradentia dataset included in because.phybase.
execute:
  eval: false
---

```r
#| include: false
library(because)
library(because.phybase)
```

## Overview

The `because` R package calls because-py transparently whenever you specify `engine = "numpyro"`.
You do not need to write any Python code — reticulate handles the bridge.
The workflow is identical to a JAGS run: same formula syntax, same `summary()` and `plot_dag()` methods.

## A benchmark model

To demonstrate the speed advantages of the NumPyro engine, we will run the phylogenetic structural equation model (Model 8) from von Hardenberg and Gonzalez-Voyer (2025) using the Rhinogradentia dataset.

```r
library(because)
library(because.phybase)

data(rhino.dat)
data(rhino.tree)

sem8_eq <- list(
    LS ~ BM,
    NL ~ BM + RS,
    DD ~ NL
)
```

### Running with JAGS (Default)

Let's start running the model with the default JAGS engine 

```r
start.time <- Sys.time()
fit_jags <- because(
    equations = sem8_eq,
    data = rhino.dat,
    structure = rhino.tree,
    id_col = "SP",
    engine = "jags", #default, so you could also omit this argument
    parallel = TRUE,
    n.cores = 3,
    n.chains = 3,
    n.iter = 12500, 
    n.burnin = 2500,
    n.thin = 10 
)
time_diff <- Sys.time() - start.time
time_diff
# Time difference of 2.284368 mins
```
*Note:* The above values for n.iter, n.burnin and n.thin are the default values for JAGS in the because package, and they were chosen to achieve an effective sample size of ~3,000 for all parameters (with 3 chains). 

### Running with NumPyro

The NumPyro engine compiles the model to JAX, leveraging Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation and the highly efficient No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS). 

```r
start.time <- Sys.time()
fit_numpyro <- because(
    equations = sem8_eq,
    data = rhino.dat,
    structure = rhino.tree,
    id_col = "SP",
    engine = "numpyro", #switch to the NumPyro engine
    parallel = TRUE,
    n.cores = 3,
    n.chains = 3,
    n.iter = 2000,
    n.warmup = 1000,
    n.thin = 1 #no thinning needed with numpyro
)
time_diff2 <- Sys.time() - start.time
time_diff2
# Time difference of 19.33303 secs
```
*Note:* The above values for n.iter, n.warmup and n.thin are the default values for NumPyro in the because package, and they were chosen to achieve an effective sample size of ~3,000 for all parameters (with 3 chains). The NumPyro engine does not require thinning due to the efficiency of the NUTS sampler, so `n.thin` is by default set to 1.

### Identical Estimates, Superior Efficiency

Both engines arrive at mathematically equivalent parameter estimates, but NumPyro's NUTS sampler is vastly more efficient per-iteration. 

To achieve an effective sample size (`n.eff`) of ~3,000 using JAGS (which defaults to `thin = 10`), we had to run `12,500` iterations, which took **approximately 137 seconds** to compute on a Macbook Air M2. with 24 GB RAM.

In contrast, NumPyro achieved the same massive `n.eff` in just **19 seconds** with only 2,000 iterations per chain!

**JAGS Output (137 seconds):**
```
                 Mean    SD Naive SE Time-series SE   2.5%    50% 97.5%  Rhat n.eff
alpha_DD        0.490 0.204    0.004          0.004  0.090  0.488 0.902 1.001  2915
alpha_LS        0.516 0.343    0.006          0.010 -0.154  0.520 1.207 1.000  1310
alpha_NL       -0.013 0.326    0.006          0.010 -0.671 -0.010 0.631 1.000  1015
beta_DD_NL      0.589 0.066    0.001          0.001  0.462  0.590 0.716 1.001  3468
beta_LS_BM      0.489 0.084    0.002          0.002  0.330  0.489 0.661 1.000  2718
beta_NL_BM      0.400 0.071    0.001          0.001  0.262  0.400 0.536 1.000  2847
beta_NL_RS      0.636 0.069    0.001          0.001  0.503  0.636 0.771 1.000  3452
lambda_DD       0.198 0.135    0.002          0.003  0.011  0.175 0.498 1.000  1600
lambda_LS       0.442 0.135    0.002          0.003  0.195  0.444 0.708 1.002  1733
lambda_NL       0.549 0.115    0.002          0.003  0.304  0.556 0.753 1.000  2038
sigma_DD_res    0.984 0.088    0.002          0.002  0.816  0.982 1.164 1.000  2586
sigma_LS_res    1.065 0.111    0.002          0.002  0.861  1.058 1.293 1.001  2510
sigma_NL_res    0.836 0.088    0.002          0.002  0.672  0.833 1.012 1.000  2882
sigma_phylo_DD  0.467 0.202    0.004          0.005  0.110  0.457 0.884 1.000  1732
sigma_phylo_LS  0.957 0.219    0.004          0.005  0.567  0.946 1.421 1.003  1752
sigma_phylo_NL  0.936 0.170    0.003          0.004  0.625  0.929 1.293 1.000  1805
sigma_total_DD  1.108 0.094    0.002          0.002  0.946  1.098 1.309 1.000  2609
sigma_total_LS  1.447 0.134    0.002          0.003  1.219  1.433 1.737 1.002  2118
sigma_total_NL  1.264 0.119    0.002          0.003  1.061  1.255 1.529 1.000  2160
```

**NumPyro Output (19 seconds):**
```
                 Mean    SD Naive SE Time-series SE   2.5%    50% 97.5%  Rhat n.eff
alpha_DD         0.477 0.188    0.003          0.004  0.118  0.472 0.859 1.002  2200
alpha_LS         0.528 0.345    0.006          0.009 -0.150  0.517 1.260 1.003  1385
alpha_NL        -0.020 0.333    0.006          0.010 -0.715 -0.015 0.639 1.000  1221
beta_DD_NL       0.597 0.067    0.001          0.001  0.467  0.599 0.726 1.000  3164
beta_LS_BM       0.486 0.086    0.002          0.002  0.315  0.485 0.661 1.000  3120
beta_NL_BM       0.402 0.071    0.001          0.001  0.265  0.402 0.537 1.000  3079
beta_NL_RS       0.631 0.069    0.001          0.001  0.497  0.633 0.771 1.000  3250
lambda_DD_phylo  0.138 0.130    0.002          0.006  0.000  0.099 0.463 1.001   540
lambda_LS_phylo  0.439 0.142    0.003          0.005  0.166  0.439 0.708 1.010   716
lambda_NL_phylo  0.557 0.115    0.002          0.004  0.305  0.567 0.755 1.000   716
sigma_DD         1.017 0.090    0.002          0.002  0.845  1.014 1.197 1.001  1431
sigma_DD_phylo   0.362 0.222    0.004          0.010  0.018  0.336 0.853 1.001   538
sigma_LS         1.075 0.117    0.002          0.003  0.863  1.071 1.317 1.006  1148
sigma_LS_phylo   0.963 0.235    0.004          0.008  0.548  0.953 1.454 1.008   876
sigma_NL         0.839 0.090    0.002          0.003  0.683  0.831 1.031 1.001  1194
sigma_NL_phylo   0.955 0.173    0.003          0.006  0.630  0.951 1.309 1.000   866
```
**A Note on Sampling Efficiency**: If you look closely at the outputs above, you will notice that while NumPyro generates massive effective sample sizes (n.eff) for the beta regression coefficients, JAGS appears slightly more efficient at sampling the variance components (sigma) and phylogenetic signals (lambda). This highlights a  difference in the respective samplers architecture: JAGS uses highly optimized, conjugate Gibbs updates for variance parameters, whereas NumPyro's NUTS (Hamiltonian Monte Carlo) uses gradient-based trajectories that must navigate complex geometries. However, because NumPyro's JAX compilation is so incredibly fast, it still generates more effective samples per second across the board. In this example, JAGS generates ~13 effective lambda samples per second and ~21.8 beta samples per second, while NumPyro generates ~34.5 effective lambda samples per second  and ~165.7 effective beta samples per second.

## Including uncertainity in trees 

NumPyro truly shines when handling more complex causal models, for example models that incorporate phylogenetic uncertainty across multiple trees (`multiPhylo`). 

While JAGS struggles with indexing into large 3D arrays of precision matrices and evaluating the determinant dynamically at every MCMC step, the `because_py` NumPyro engine implementation in `because`  handles multiple trees marginalising out the discrete tree index and evaluating all trees simultaneously during the NUTS trajectory using the `DiscreteHMCGibbs` sampler of `NumPyro`. This approach is orders of magnitude faster than the naive JAGS implementation, which requires a separate MCMC run for each tree.

In the following example, we simulate 10 trees for the same 100 species and run the same model across all 10 trees simultaneously using again both the JAGS and the NumPyro engines.

```r
# Simulate 10 trees for the 100 species using the 'ape' package
library(ape)
data(rhino.tree)
set.seed(123)

# Create an empty list to hold our trees
trees_list <- list()

# Generate 10 trees by perturbing the branch lengths of the original tree
for(i in 1:10) {
  perturbed_tree <- rhino.tree
  
  # Multiply each branch length by a random value between 0.8 and 1.2 (+/- 20% noise)
  noise <- runif(length(perturbed_tree$edge.length), min = 0.8, max = 1.2)
  perturbed_tree$edge.length <- perturbed_tree$edge.length * noise
  
  trees_list[[i]] <- perturbed_tree
}

# Convert the list to a formal multiPhylo object
multi_tree <- structure(trees_list, class = "multiPhylo")

# Assign the exact same species names to the tips of our simulated trees
for (i in 1:10) {
  multi_tree[[i]]$tip.label <- rhino.tree$tip.label
}

#run the multi tree model using JAGS (this will be very slow)

system.time <- Sys.time()
fit_multi_numpyro <- because(
    equations = sem8_eq,
    data = rhino.dat,
    structure = multi_tree,
    id_col = "SP",
    engine = "jags",
    parallel = TRUE,
    n.cores = 3,
)
time_diff_multi_jg <- Sys.time() - system.time
time_diff_multi_jg
# Time difference of 9.70127 mins

#Now run the same multi-tree model using the NumPyro engine (this will be very fast) 
system.time <- Sys.time()
fit_multi_numpyro <- because(
    equations = sem8_eq,
    data = rhino.dat,
    structure = multi_tree,
    id_col = "SP",
    engine = "numpyro",
    parallel = TRUE,
    n.cores = 3,
)
time_diff_multi_py <- Sys.time() - system.time
time_diff_multi_py
# Time difference of 21.40381 secs
```

## Engine Feature Comparison

The `because` R package abstracts the underlying sampler so you can easily switch between them. Choose the engine that best fits your data and model features.

| Feature | JAGS (Default) | NIMBLE | NumPyro (Python) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Distributions** |Gaussian, Poisson, Binomial, NegBinomial, ZIP, ZINB, Ordinal, Multinomial | As JAGS | As JAGS |
| **d-separation testing** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Latent variables (MAGs)** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Interaction terms** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Polynomials** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Deterministic nodes** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Multi-scale models (Hierarchical)**| Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Priors** | Extensive support | Extensive support | Standard priors |
| **Random Effects** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Phylogenetic Models** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **multiPhylo (Uncertainty)**| Slow | Fast | **Fast (Rao-Blackwellized)** |
| **Measurement Error (SE)** | Yes | Yes | **No** |
| **Repeated Measures** | Yes | Yes | **No** |
| **Performance (Large Data)**| Slow | Fast | **Extremely Fast (JAX)** |
| **Hardware** | CPU only | CPU only | **CPU / GPU / TPU** |

## Inspecting the generated NumPyro model code

The generated NumPyro model is stored as an executable Python string:

```r
cat(fit_numpyro$numpyro_code)
```

This is useful for verification, customisation, and teaching.
